title: Sand Land

content.text: This open-world RPG is a flawless expansion of the Sand Land universe, which will delight fans of the franchise. However, it may have relied a little too much on its source, and left the gameplay lacking.
A Land Worth Expanding

Sand Land is a short manga series and anime, so the game is perfectly positioned to expand that universe, and that's what it does best. Visiting some of the show's locations in-game gave me a whole new perspective on their scale and importance through exploration and side quests. And just like the anime, it goes beyond Sand Land into Forest Land as well, meaning the game features two big areas to explore.

That said, both of these areas are populated by a lot of copy-pasted assets. The more I explored, the more I recognized a lot of rooms and puzzles in side activities, like ruins, grottos or mountains. They're not just the same type of location, they are literally the same.
A Desert of Innovation

The game has a lot of gameplay systems: melee combat, vehicular combat, races and stealth. Together they offer good variety, but each one is as simple as it gets. Combat is the familiar light and strong attack system, with a dodge roll and occasional special attacks. Races are basic time trials. Despite having other racers on the track, their position doesn't matter. And stealth is just the usual crouching to avoid making noise, with a basic takedown button. No distractions, no bodies found, no AI manipulation. Even getting found means simply respawning somewhere else and trying again.

The biggest problem is that nothing evolves as much as you would expect. Even though it's an RPG, with levels and talents to unlock, gameplay during the first hour is not distinct enough from hour 40. The numbers are bigger, and you'll have more special attacks, but you'll still be using the same strategies, and often still pressing the same buttons. So for as many systems as the game has, over time it becomes boring.
Tanks for Playing

That said, most of your time with the game is spent in a vehicle, and that's easily the best part of the entire package. The game offers a good number of vehicle types, all of which can be customized with different weapons and visuals, and gameplay is very different depending on the vehicle you're riding. There are hovercrafts, tanks, motorcycles and even a mech-suit. Individually, their gameplay is still very simplistic, but since you can quickly swap vehicles mid-fight, it keeps the game fun.

Not that it's challenging. Enemies can't lead their shots, so driving sideways and shooting will always work regardless of who you're up against and what you set the difficulty to. It's the gameplay's saving grace.

verdict: With all of its gameplay systems, Sand Land feels like a jack of all trades, but master of none. It relies too much on the show it's based on, failing to stand on its own.

title: Naruto: The Broken Bond

content.text: The successor to one of the greatest Naruto games of all time, Rise of a Ninja, arrives with an increased roster and multiple playable characters in story mode, but ends up padding the experience with filler quests, leaving a stain on what could've been the perfect sequel.
Filler Clone Jutsu

This time, the story isn't just played through the eyes of Naruto, you can play with a variety of characters such as Shikamaru, Sasuke and more. Each one of these has their own exploration abilities that allow you to clear obstacles or reach places no one else can.

But more isn't better in this case. Many of these abilities are used to collect coins, or to solve basic "pull this lever" puzzles, which increases the padding in this game. The filler is everywhere, so even though the idea of playing with multiple characters is great, the execution buries it. Besides, little to nothing was done to correct the terrible movement feel, which remains the same from Rise of a Ninja.
Living the Genjutsu

Filler aside, this game still does canon story moments like no other games do. It opens really strong with the Third Hokage's passing, as you get to see a destroyed Konoha in mourning. Later, you live through Sasuke's experience while he's trapped in his brother's Tsukuyomi, or struggle to complete mini games as Naruto learns the Rasengan. In another game, these moments would be a cutscene, but The Broken Bond has no qualms about giving you control and immersing you in this experience.

The game no longer uses the original anime cutscenes. Every cinematic is now in engine, but it still has the original show's soundtrack, and the CG work is really good, so the loss barely registers.

Push past the filler and the story mode is still some of the best the Naruto games have ever offered.
Broken Combos

Whether it's fighting through story mode or online against other humans, the basics of combat are the same, but it feels more punishing. It's still a 3D fighting game, where every character has some combos for you to learn, and between two to three jutsu you can use to damage the opponent or buff yourself.

The roster is bigger, which means more variety in movesets, but the main changes from Rise of a Ninja are: substitutions can't be mashed so easily, and combos have huge gaps.

Substitutions are combo breaker mechanics, and they're still very cheap to use, meaning the game is still very much a substitution festival, only the skill requirement for you to do so is much greater since you can't mash it. But the change to combo routes is by far the most frustrating. The big gaps in many of the routes allow opponents to sidestep and punish you, even while getting hit. I understand the value of having some frametraps here and there, but when most of your combos aren't real, it makes you question what the developers were going for.

Just like Rise of a Ninja, it feels designed for single player first and multiplayer second. Still fun to play with friends, as long as no one gets too good at it.

verdict: A sequel that does more than Rise of a Ninja without doing it better. The filler and the combat changes make the once tight experience something you need to push through to see the moments worth reaching.

title: My Hero: One's Justice

content.text: The first My Hero Academia console game: My Hero One's Justice. An Arena Fighter that tries to stand out with a more technical approach and deep character movesets, but ends up with poor game feel and a lack of basic features.
Plus Ultra, Minus Feel

It's a 1v1 Arena Fighter, where each player can pick 2 assist characters backing you up. The game features 21 playable characters, all with unique movesets that include basic attacks and quirk abilities. Each quirk is a faithful interpretation of the character's powers from the show, resulting in a fighting game with different archetypes for each character in the roster.

Variety suffers the most with the assist system. Since they can be summoned while getting hit to act as combo breaks, your choices narrow significantly in competitive play, though casual matches still reward experimenting with the full roster.

That said, the whole game feels very floaty for a few reasons. Battles can transition from the ground to the walls, in a way that makes no sense in the show the game is adapting, and completely defies physics. The game is also heavy on recovery in most of its actions, even regular dashes have recovery, so characters feel heavy and not as snappy as you'd want. There are also some extremely long knockdowns at the end of every combo, which kill momentum and compound the sluggishness.

On one hand, I'd love to see more Arena Fighters commit to this level of moveset depth. More quality movesets over quantity of characters. But on the other hand, game feel should still be a priority, and that was neglected here.
Solo Hero

There are 3 single player modes: Story, Mission and Arcade.

Arcade is as straightforward as it gets. Pick a character, fight against 6 opponents, unlock a reward.

Story Mode is disappointingly thin. It covers the My Hero story from the Sports Festival to the battle between All Might and All For One. All of it is retold in slideshows, and the gameplay is simple CPU battles.

Fortunately, mission mode is solid. It's like an Arcade Mode, but each mission has special conditions, and your characters level up the more you use them. It's a faux RPG game mode that's really challenging and has some replayability, as you get to choose different paths to the final boss of each mission, and every path has different rewards.

Customization runs through all three modes as the shared progression system, as most of the rewards are customization options for your characters, and carry into offline or online matches.
Online Villains

This game has the best delay-based netcode I have ever experienced. The quality of games against opponents from the other side of the globe is really good, it feels like witchcraft. Since this is a game from 2018 with 2 sequels now, finding a match is near impossible at this point, but if you have friends to play with, distance won't be a problem with how good the netcode is.

However, online is missing a lot of basic features that are standard in most multiplayer games:
- Lobbies only hold 2 players at a time (no group lobbies)
- No rage quit penalties
- No loss of points in Ranked Matches

This means that winning or losing always ranks you up, making this less of a Ranked Match and more of a "Grind Match".

verdict: Despite committing to more character variety and moveset depth, One's Justice comes out with poor gameplay feel and lack of basic features. However, if this is the start of a new series, I would love to see them build on this foundation.

title: Reunion of Memories

content.text: Before the Straw Hats depart from Waford into their next adventure, one final party by the campfire is cut short. Lim is kidnapped and the crew is trapped in the world of memories. But things are very different this time around...
An Ancient Plot Hole

Yes, you will have to revisit Alabasta, Water Seven and Dressrosa (they skipped Marineford for some reason). This time, a Dark Lim is handing out Black Cubes that increase the power of several villains. The result? Alabasta is now ruled by Crocodile, Lucci kidnapped Mr. Iceberg, and so on.

This DLC's story dives into Lim's past, Waford's history and their relationship with the Ancient Weapon, uncovering its true nature (turns out, its power is not about controlling the weather). This expansion answered many questions I had, but the way it ties up some loose ends felt a little too convenient. If you don't think too much about it, I'm sure it's fine. But as soon as you start asking questions, a lot of it falls apart. It's a cool story, but it pokes some holes in its own plot.
Finally, A Worthy Challenge

At launch, the lack of difficulty was one of the main criticisms. The developers heard that, and so combat isn't just more challenging, it's been meaningfully expanded.

Almost every encounter has special conditions now: protect the royal guard, defeat the marines in one turn, defeat the captain without attacking their subordinates, etc.

This was the first time I actually felt challenged in One Piece Odyssey, and with the addition of these conditions, encounters also feel more dynamic.

On top of that, you're not just fighting against old bosses (like Crocodile and Rob Lucci). They made a few new boss fights as well, while giving One Piece fans some good fan service. After all, it's the world of memory... every One Piece character is fair game.
Choose your path

The DLC also introduces branching paths. At certain points, you can choose which path you want to take. These have no effect on the ending, they're two ways of arriving at the same destination, but the journey can change significantly.

This DLC actually has two endings, but triggering them mostly has to do with collectibles, not the choices you make. The two endings, however, don't do much to justify a second playthrough, as they only slightly change a cutscene close to the end of the story, leaving the ending's meaning completely intact.

verdict: The DLC finally unleashes the full potential of Odyssey's combat system, delivering a challenge that's actually fun to overcome. No matter how many plot holes it pokes in its own story.

title: One Piece Odyssey

content.text: One Piece goes turn-based RPG with Odyssey, a game with an original setting that's actually incredible if you're a One Piece fan, with a solid idea for a combat system. But in typical anime game fashion, comes with a few caveats that are hard to ignore.
Not Just Another Island

The Straw Hats shipwreck on the island of Waford, a game original land with incredible lore, inhabited only by Lim and Adio (two game original characters) and a few non-human creatures. Discovering this island's past and the story with Lim and Adio quickly becomes the game's main attraction, as this island houses an Ancient Weapon that triggers massive storms. It's a deep dive into many themes that are One Piece fan-favourites: Sky Islands, ancient tribes and Ancient Weapons.

Soon upon arrival, the Straw Hats lose their powers. So as they're getting to know Lim and Adio, they are also diving into their past adventures in the World of Memories, reliving the stories of Alabasta, Water Seven, Marineford and Dressrosa. These aren't one-to-one with how they happened in the show, though. Memories can be fuzzy, and events may change. But what's impressive here is how fully realized each one of these areas is. The capital of Alubarna, complete with the Palace and Mausoleum. Water Seven's gorgeous canals. A small town in Marineford shaken by the great Paramount War. And the scale of the Doflamingo destruction on Dressrosa. All of these really impressed me as a One Piece fan. Never have I seen these places in such detail, and it was incredible to discover them. If only I could've done it at my own pace...
One Pace... Please Help!

True to One Piece, this is a game with a lot of pacing issues, that range from storytelling to quest design and gameplay mechanics.

Small, outdated gameplay hiccups start piling up. Switching characters requires a full fade to black. Clearing a common obstacle will lock you out of player control to play a small animation every time. Moving from one area to another can take multiple loading screens.

But it's not just the small things, the four areas are incredibly unbalanced in terms of playtime, with Alabasta being the biggest problem. Most of this arc is played before unlocking Fast Travel, with quest design that requires you to not only cross a huge desert on foot, but to constantly backtrack as well, across multiple quests. The story also moves very slowly at the start, which ends up feeling like unnecessary padding. Ultimately it makes the start of the game feel like a total drag.
No Trust In Your Nakama

Odyssey's combat feels original at first. It has some familiar turn-based tropes, like character types and elemental weaknesses, but stands out for placing characters in different zones. Characters can't move away from these zones until all the enemies in their zone are defeated, which will force you to strategize how best to place the Straw Hats in each encounter. Every crew member has their own type and elemental attacks, but also specialize in either single target attacks, area attacks, close range or long range (as in, attacks to a different area than the one they're standing in).

It's a really cool idea, but also feels like it didn't fully develop, as it quickly falls into the background after that initial surprise. Once you figure out that you can swap crewmembers' positions, as many times as you want, without spending a turn, it feels like you broke the whole game by using a basic mechanic. But even without exploiting this, the game never challenges you enough to fully explore this battle system. It's another anime game that doesn't trust its playerbase to have any brainpower, and doesn't offer any difficulty options. It's not until you're done with the story and choose to tackle the post-game content that it offers any meaningful challenge.

In the end, the combat system runs out of steam, and what kept gameplay fun was seeing all the attack animations they did for the whole crew, as well as the incredible enemy designs, all with their own attack animations.

verdict: An incredible setting that will delight every One Piece fan, with some cool ideas for turn-based combat, undermined by its low difficulty and pacing issues.

title: Gogeta (SSJ4)

content.text:
Gogeta (SSJ4) was believed to be the final character of FighterZ for a long time. So they didn't hold back. The character lacks big projectiles, but has the unique trait of having 2 air dashes for every jump, a level up gimmick and even an insta-kill.

Besides, they made a Super Saiyan 4 Vegeta model just to see him fuse during his intro. Now that's dedication.
Party-hameha

Gogeta has a fake kamehameha that shoots party confetti, and it's the funniest command grab in the whole game. He also has a frame 4 counter attack and a DP that gives him a Dragon Ball every time he uses it.

For more mixup, his command normals give him high and low options. To get in closer to the opponent, he has a projectile invulnerable lariat, and a spinning kick to knock them down.
"I Only Need One Finger"

He also has a taunt that will level him up every time he uses it. Every level will buff his Level 1 and his Level 3 Supers

At level 7 however, he gains an insta-kill. His Big Bang Kamehameha will delete a character, no matter how much health they have left.

He also has a Dramatic Finish with Gogeta Blue where he can win or lose (they animated both outcomes).

verdict: With how little screen time the character has, but with how iconic it is among fans, they did a fantastic job.

title: Super Baby 2

content.text: I thought I'd seen zoners in Dragon Ball FighterZ with Frieza and Ginyu... but then Baby came out. They built this character to turn this game into a bullet hell. All of his ki blasts return from off screen when they whiff, and some of them even return if they hit you or make you block. Best ki blasts in the entire game.
Tuffle Parasite

His specials support his zoning and give him crazy combo loops as well. Starting with a very easy to use, anti air spinning move that shoots ki blasts at the end of the spin. And yes, these ki blasts will also return, even if your opponent blocks them.

If you manage to take down anyone on the enemy team, you can then summon them with another special move. They'll perform their assist attack (A, B or C assist depending on the attack button you use)

His other specials are a bit more familiar: a beam, a diving punch, and an anti air grab.
Monke Time

Baby has a stanrdard Level 1 and Level 3. Both beautifully animated, but easy to use in pretty much every combo.

But he also has a unique Level 3 super that activates from close range, with a guard point, and has him transforming into the Great Ape. At first, the cinematic attack has Baby unleashing a big beatdown, but this super evolves into a Galick Gun if you've lost any member on your team during the match, which also buffs its damage.

verdict: I hate zoners with a passion, but even I can't deny that this is an archetype that FighterZ was lacking. He feels nothing like the other zoners, and has so many cool looking moves that it might even convert some zoning haters out there into maining him. Not me though, my zoning hatred is still too strong.

title: Master Roshi

content.text: This is... hands down... the weirdest character to play in Dragon Ball FighterZ. Mostly because they chose to take away a very basic mechanic: the Super Dash. Instead, he has a reverse kamehameha, so he can fly in a straight line. But this increases the skill floor required to play this character like crazy.
Hard to Master... Roshi

They gave him a ton of special moves too, so the character feels very complete, despite being so hard to master.

Thunder Shock Surprise is an unblockable anti air that will cover most of the screen, making jumping really dangerous against Roshi.

He also has an armored reversal where he'll buff himself up briefly before striking you

And he has a really quick attack where he jumps and does the splits.

He also has some more familiar special moves, like a Kamehameha, a lariat with a cinematic attack on hit, and a command grab.
GET IN THE JAR!

They gave him the Mafuba, where he seals an opponent in a jar! If this super kills them, he covers the lid, and that's it! If it doesn't, you force a snapback, bringing in the next character on your opponent's team.

He also has a more regular Level 1 super with a melee activation, and the option to extend it into a Level 2

And of course, the original kamehameha as his Level 3 super.

verdict: The most technical character in the game won't be for everyone, but no one can deny how original, unique and how true to the character they made him.

title: Goku (Ultra Instinct)

content.text: UI Goku needed to be powerful... and he does feel that way! This is one of the best anime character into fighting game interpretations I've ever seen. Not only did they give him a TON of stuff, it feels and looks incredible too.
Whis Would Be Proud

Before we even get to his specials, by simply walking forward he dodges all ki blasts! He's got a command counter attack that automatically takes him behind the opponent (and it's the coolest looking counter in the whole game). But he's not just about counters, he's got a ton of screen control with his special button plus different directions.

When it comes to special attacks... I think it's better if I just list them:
- A Kamehameha
- Dash past the opponent that plays a cinematic attack on hit
- An attack flurry for knockdowns
- A vertical beam that's also a DP
- A unique invulnerable wake up option
- A punch that gives him plus frames
- A command grab
- A frame 4 reversal
- Invincible flips that advance on screen (and cross up the opponent), that can be followed up with any of these options

They were not joking around. They wanted you to feel the power of Ultra Instinct.
Getting The Hang of This

No, he doesn't have enough counters, as one of his Level 1 supers is yet another counter attack move. He also has a standard Level 1 that actually be extended into a Level 2 Super, and one of the most gorgeous Level 3 supers in the entire game.

verdict: It's not just the amount of moves, it's how close they feel to what Ultra Instinct is, and what it represents in Dragon Ball. It's a perfect interpretation of this character.

title: Kefla

content.text: Ridiing the Tournament of Power hype, Kefla comes out with incredible visuals, but a somewhat basic moveset.
The Universe 6 Saiyans

Kefla's signature is a dashing move that always goes to the opponent's back. Good for mixups, neutral, and even some combo conversions. That said, it's also a simple dash, so her signature doesn't feel that special.

She also has a big projectile (that's projectile invulnerable) she'll spin on her hands to get a sliding knockdown, but holding the button will extend its duration and throw a projectile. This is a pretty unique special move as well, but doesn't give you a lot of new stuff in the way you use it.

Her remaining specials are pretty standard: a projectile and a low hitting strike.
Candy Cane Supers

She's got three Level 1 Supers:
- A standard projectile (kind of underwheling)
- A chase with projectiles carrying the opponent to the ground (good for follow up supers)
- A Hellzone Grenade.

I would be impressed, if this wasn't the third Hellzone in the game at this point. She's also got a level 3 Super that's beautifully animated and hits fullscreen.

She also has a dramatic finish against Goku Blue (who transforms into UI Goku Sign). She does lose.

verdict: Despite feeling a bit basic, Kefla still brings some unique moves to Dragon Ball FighterZ and incredible visuals like always.

title: Broly (Super)

content.text: Broly is what I call a Raging Grappler. He's a grappler, sure (you'll see it in the next paragraph), but he always does it with a lot of rage. And it comes off in his normals, as he can link up to four crouching medium attacks, stomping the ground over and over, and his jumping down heavy is a cinematic command grab. Hope you like yelling, 'cause Broly doesn't stop.
Grappling Saiyan

He has 6 Command Grabs in total (yes, counting the jumping down heavy I just mentioned). A grab for every occasion: standing opponents, crouching opponents, jumping opponents, a EX grab for when you're not sure, an OTG grab to extend combos... Broly be grabbing folks.

Besides that, he's got a big ground pound (that reaches fullscreen), and an angled projectile that can go into a cinematic follow up if you land it up close.
Take Your Shirt Off!

Broly has FIVE SUPERS. If not for Gogeta's ridiculous six supers, this would've been a new record. But unlike Gogeta, Broly's main gimmick actually exists here, in his level 3.

By landing his level 3 in a match, Broly rip his shirt off for the rest of the game, buffing his damage (and gaining passive meter regeneration after a recent patch). So this is a priority for Broly players. Besides, it's probably the best Level 3 Super Animation in the whole game, and that's impressive. I do wish the Legendary Super Saiyan transformation was permanent (he reverts back to normal at the end of the super, minus the shirt), but the gimmick is still incredible.

Besides that he has three Level 1 Supers:
- one that goes horizontally fullscreen on the ground
- another one in the air that can be extended into a level 2
- and a reversal that can extend the combo by himself

He also has a dramatic finish against Gogeta Blue. It's gorgeous, but he does lose in the end.

verdict: Sucks to not have a permanent Legendary SSJ transformation, but they captured his rage so well, and with how unique the moveset is, I have to give it the perfect score.

title: Gogeta (SSGSS)

content.text: They wanted this character to feel powerful... and they succeeded. They gave him so much, in terms of specials and supers, but even just hitting the special attack button throws a beam! The dude is so powerful, that instead of regular ki blasts, a full kamehameha comes out. It's crazy!
The Complete Fusion
He has Dash kicks, a DP, Clones that throw beams and a Command Grab. This character has everything!
Super Super Saiyan Super

He has... SIX SUPERS. SIX! The most Supers any character has ever had.

A grounded level 1, a grounded level 2, two jumping level 1s, a level 3 and a level 5. Supers for every situation, spending as many bars as you want each time.

If that wasn't enough, this is also the DLC character with the most dramatic finishes (vs Broly Super, vs Janemba and vs SSJ4 Gogeta), and they are arguably the best in the game.

verdict: Gogeta Blue had to be a strong character. But capturing power isn't always easy in a fighting game, without making a character immediately broken. They succeeded by making him very complete, giving him a ton of supers, and some absolutely gorgeous cinematics to go along with it.

title: Janemba

content.text: When I think Janemba I think Sword and Portals. And they delivered! Janemba doesn't always fight with with a sword, but has plenty of sword normals (mostly his heavy attacks), and his regular ki blasts are shot in the opponent's face, as he puts his hand through a portal and tracks their position.
Teleporting Hell

Janemba's teleport is iconic, and they nailed its visual effect, but more than that, it's also a cool ability, since you can direct where you wanna teleport, and it has invincibility. So it's a great defensive move, as well as a great mixup move (for cross ups)

He also has a unique trap special move. He shoots an orb that moves across the entire stage, and you can choose when to detonate it. Great for pressure, neutral and even combos!

The rest of his specials aren't so unique, but complete his moveset nice: a big sword slice, a command grab and a projectile parry.
Janembeats

He's got two level 1 supers, useful for different situations, and a level 3 with an animation that looks like a song's waveform. Janemba quickly became FighterZ resident DJ thanks to this!

He has a dramatic finish against Gogeta Blue, but it's on the losing side, so you probably don't wanna see that as Janemba.

verdict: Nailed the visuals, and his unique moveset really fits the character we knew from the show.

title: Goku (GT)

content.text: Another Goku... well at least this one is tiny. And has some interesting gimmicks once again!
Wait... Naruto?

Hard to say what his signature move even is, since he's got so many unique specials. The "Rasengan" is good for neutral, pressure and knockdowns, the reverse Kamehameha is good for both combos and neutral. Not to mention all the unique stuff he can do with his power pole (attack or move around the stage as well).

He's also got some more basic specials, like a lariat and a Kamehameha, though this time he can only shoot it at an angle.
Super Evolution 4

His level one and level 3 supers evolve as you lose teammates. It's like Base Goku, but now applies to his level 3 as well.

His level one is a basic Kamehameha. Normally he'll go Super Saiyan before shooting it, but after losing at least 1 team member, he'll go Super Saiyan 3, dealing increased damage.

And his level 3 is a Dragon Fist, that he does in his normal form at first, but goes Super Saiyan 4 after at least one teammate goes down. And it's beautifully animated.

GT Goku also has one the coolest Spirit Bomb in the game. He can combo into it from some routes, and combo after the Spirit Bomb lands. It opens up his routes like crazy.

verdict: Power pole, reverse kamehameha, evolving supers, this character is cool as hell!

title: Videl

content.text: The only character in FighterZ that doesn't have a Reflect. She's a very complete character, that can summon the Great Saiyaman to shoot ki blasts for her.
No Energy, No Problem

Videl is another rekka character. This time the rekka has some cross up and command grab options. Not as powerful of a rekka as Android 17's, but she can pull off a lot of these moves without the rekka, making her very unpredictable on block.

She also has a DP. That's always nice to have.
A True Martial Artist

She has FOUR SUPERS!
Two Level 1s, both very unique, as one of them is a Kick that you can combo from (if you put it early enough in the combo), and the other one is a parry, where the Great Saiyaman punches the opponent. This buffs all of the Great Saiyaman moves for the rest of the match!

She also has Two Level 3s. One of them works normally (thank goodness), and the other one you can do by holding down a button on wake up. Like Frieza's wake up super.

It's also worth noting she's the only character in the whole game that has a real outfit change (not just colors). You can pick between short hair or pigtails Videl.

verdict: An incredible character held back by the decision to take a core mechanic away from her moveset. The lack of a reflect forever left this character in a weird place.

title: Jiren

content.text:
His Heavy attack button is a grab? He can anti air with his ki blast? What's going on here?!

Jiren looks like a very weird character at first. But it doesn't take long to find out how basic he can be.
Pride Parry

Jiren's signature are his parries and counter attacks. He has a low parry, a high parry, an EX parry (that covers both for meter), and a projectile parry. It can be very scary to attack a good Jiren. If they can read you, you're cooked.

Other than that, his specials are pretty straightforward. A shoulder charge and a deadly stare (it's what it sounds like... he stares at you very hard).
Super Trooper

Two Level 1 Supers (a projectile and a punch), and a Level 3 where he... punches really hard? Underwhelming supers honestly.

Jiren didn't release with a Dramatic Finish, but got one if he loses against Goku and Frieza.

verdict: Honestly impressive how much they made with such a straightforward character, but at the end of the day, he feels a bit too simple. I like the parry gimmick though, it can make for some big brain plays.

title: Cooler

content.text: Cooler feels like a character Frankensteined from others. It's like... none of his moves feel particularly new but... seeing them all together, he still feels unique?
Bigger Brother

A big dive kick, a big DP, a big beam. All things we've seen before, but Cooler's big body is gonna do them in big style.

He does have a unique special move, that's fullscreen low that picks up opponents off the ground, and a command normal that lunges half screen and is projectile invul! I'd say these are probably his signature moves, but it's hard to nail it to a single special.
Supernova Counter

Two Level 1 Supers, both very easy to use, and a Level 3 that can be used normally or as a parry. No safe jumping Cooler if he's got 3 bars of meter.

Unfortunately, along with Android 17, he's the first character that does not bring a new Dramatic Finish to the game.

verdict: A character with big normals and a bunch of familiar specials mixed together. He feels very good to play, but doesn't add much in terms of variety to the current roster.

title: Android 17

content.text: The MVP of the Tournament of Power arrives as a bit of a mix between Android 18 and... Yamcha?
Dance Baby Dance

His biggest special is a rekka with a ton of options (like Yamcha), but this time, instead of having a lot of left/right mix, it's more of high/low, air to ground carries, shield baits and loads of plus frames... yeah it's a Rekka with some insane options.

The shield is especially strong because it can be followed up with an attack, immediately punishing any buttons, and that can be used as an assist.

His other special is very forgettable. He can jump to the wall. Woopie.
What's His Super Again?

He has two Level 1 Supers and a Level 3. While both Level 1 are taken from the show, the Level 3 is an original move from Xenoverse 2. I didn't think they would need to go game original with the MVP, but they did...

This is also the first character (together with Cooler) that didn't bring a Dramatic Finish.

verdict: Godlike character competitively, but the whole moveset is a rekka and a shield assist, leaving everything else to fall a bit.

title: Vegeta

content.text: As a Vegeta, he shares a lot of normals with the other two Vegetas in the game. And that would be fine, if he actually had anything else that felt unique about him...
He's a Saiyan

He's got a super dash kick (like SSJ Vegeta), an Overhead Knee attack (kind of a mix between Tien and Adult Gohan's backfist), and a Destructo Disk (like Kril... actually Krillin's is a super, nevermind)

The most unique special he brings is a grab that can pickup opponents from the ground. The first OTG move of the game.
Elite Simplicity

Galick Gun as a level 1 and the move that finished Nappa as a level 3. That's it for supers. They're good, the level 3 oki has always been incredible. The character is very strong. Just doesn't feel super unique.

He has a Dramatic Finish against Goku, but as Vegeta you probably don't wanna see it, as it would mean you lost the fight...

verdict: Very strong, very complete character, but did not feel very unique. Honestly, if he didn't have the ki blast loops and level 3 oki, he would feel like a dupe.

title: Goku

content.text: Another Goku huh... well this one, despite sharing a lot of normals with the other Gokus, actually has some incredibly unique gimmicks. This is the first true anchor of Dragon Ball FighterZ.
Give Me Your Energy

Goku can charge up a spirit bomb projectile up to 3 times before shooting it. It's a very powerful projectile but needs to be charged up first. It's very unique in this game.

The other specials aren't so unique. A shoulder charge and a command grab. Nice to have, but we've seen stuff like this before.
Kaioken Times... Math!

Here's where it gets interesting. His Level 1 Super evolves as your team dies. Kaioken starts as a rekka with 3 follow ups, evolving into 5 and then 9, with the option of finishing it all with a Kamehameha for an extra bar of meter. Goku's Super gets stronger the less characters are on your team.

On top of that, his Level 3 is a Super Spirit Bomb that deals incredible damage. It falls slower, so it won't connect in a lot of regular combos, but that's why he's great as an anchor. Set it up with another character, follow up with a Spirit Bomb... it's TOD town!

To add to peak, he has TWO Dramatic Finishes: Goku vs Vegeta and Goku vs Frieza. He wins both of them of course.

verdict: Honestly, the only downside is it being another Goku in a game full of Gokus. Otherwise he actually brings a lot of new stuff to the game, and stands out as a very unique character.

title: Zamasu (Fused)

content.text: The almighty Zamasu arrives in FighterZ with godly damage and divine setups. A unique character for sure, but one that revolves a bit too much around one gimmick.
Flight of the Gods

8 direction free form flight. That's Zamasu's signature! He can activate flight and hover at will around the stage. He has other specials, that he can perform on the ground or while flying, but they're a lot more boring.

Well, maybe I'm being too harsh. He does have these orbs of lightning that automatically shoot after a delay that are also very unique, and can be cool for covering gaps in blockstrings or extending combos.
Divine Supers

He's got two Level 1 Supers. One of them is a simple orb. The other can be used for setups or with certain knockdowns to extend combos. It's very similar to Piccolo's Hellzone Grenade.

He's also got a gorgeous Level 3 Super.

Zamasu has two Dramatic Finishes (against Vegito and Trunks), but he loses both of them. So if you're playing Zamasu and see these, it's probably bad news.

verdict: A very cool character for sure, but outside of flight nothing feels super unique.

title: Vegito (SSGSS)

content.text: This character is weird. His normals are very unique, so they won't feel very familiar. But sometimes that's a good thing. It's what makes characters unique.
Swiss Army Earrings

His special moves have a little bit of everything. Projectiles, a parry, some melee kicks and a grab. The fusion always felt like a very complete character, even if they're missing high/low mixup, the amount of tools and neutral skips they have kept them menacing throughout the game's lifespan.
Final Kamehameha

Two level 1 supers and a level 3 that can be extended with a fourth bar of meter, give you many options on how to finish combos from every situation. The animation on Final Kamehameha is one of the best the game has seen so far (and that's saying something), and the Dramatic Finish he has against Zamasu is one of the funniest in the game.

verdict: If you're willing to learn this character, he'll reward you for the time you put into him. If you're looking to auto-pilot with your current muscle memory though, maybe skip this one.

title: Broly

content.text: This is what happens when you can go absolutely crazy! A unique playstyle that fits a monster of a character.
Armored Express

Broly is an armored monster. He's got an armored lariat, two armored command grabs, an armored projectile, and can even setup an anti-projectile shield.

He's not just scary because he's big, but his armor makes him scarry to challenge against, but also block against thanks to
Green Explosions Everywhere

Broly has two level 3 supers! They kinda look the same, but functionally they're very different, as one of them throws a very slow moving orb that can be used for pressure or for solo combo extensions in certain situations. With enough meter, this character is a killing machine.

Plus, he also brings two cinematic Dramatic Finishes, against Adult Gohan and Goku! He does lose both of them unfortunately...

verdict: Nailing a character's personality and fighting style this well, while at the same time adding something so unique to a fighting game is a rare thing indeed. Perfect DLC.

title: Bardock

content.text: A beginner friendly character whose biggest problem is playing a little too similar to all the other saiyans.
A lariat to remember

The lariat quickly became Bardock's signature move in FighterZ, you could say that's the move that defines the character in this game. A quick attack that moves him across the stage, quickly starting his rushdown pressure.

His other specials are an overhead rolling crush, good for guaranteeing knockdowns on every combo, and Tyrant Lancer, a punch with a big startup that gives you plus frames on block.
Cinematic Pride

Bardock comes with 2 level one supers that can be linked into each other (essentially giving him a level 2), and a beautifully animated level 3. But that's not all!

He also comes with 2 Dramatic Finishes against Frieza. One for his victory and another one for his loss. This is the only place where you can see Bardock go Super Saiyan unfortunately. That transformation is not available to him during gameplay.

verdict: A very safe start to Season 1 DLC. The character suffers a little from being too straightforward, but makes it up with 2 Dramatic Finishes and a very fun lariat.

title: Dragon Ball FighterZ

content.text:
I love Fighting Games. I love Dragon Ball.

FighterZ is the best thing that could've happened to someone like me. A refreshing take on tag fighters that became many people's first fighting game, and ended up influencing the whole genre.
A Welcoming Tag Fighter
Online Warriors

Despite the game launching with delay-based netcode, an update years later added rollback, which transformed the online experience.

We still don't have crossplay (and most likely never will), but the online netcode and game modes (Lobbies, Ranked and Casual Matches) have kept the game alive and well so far.

There are also occasional cooperative events where 3 players can tackle a series of powerful enemies. Unfortunately this game mode is still tied to delay-based netcode, and has been plagued with various issues since launch. Don't get me wrong, it's a super cool mode, just hard to enjoy sometimes
Hyperbolic Single Player

This game's single player offering is honestly underrated.

While its combo trials are underwhelming, thanks to how difficult the CPU can get, both the Arcade and Story Modes can actually be satisfying to clear.

Story is actually filled with a ton of unique cinematic interactions between characters that never met in the show, while telling an original story centred on the game's original character: Android 21. The narrative isn't bad as far as filler stories go in Dragon Ball, though it can be confusing since it's told from 3 different perspectives, and main events don't always line up.

The reason this offering gets overlooked is that the hard difficulties only unlock after you beat the game on "Normal", which is incredibly boring. The CPU just isn't challenging enough, so the task feels incredibly repetitive, and most players end up dropping it before getting to the good stuff.

verdict: Despite its polarizing single player offering, Dragon Ball FighterZ is the tag fighter that defined an entire genre, and my favorite Dragon Ball game of all time.

title: Naruto: Rise of a Ninja

content.text: Rise of a Ninja's main goal is to make you feel what it's like to live in Naruto's world, through his perspective. Many Naruto games have tried this, but very few have been as successful at doing so as Rise of a Ninja. This open world action adventure game is incredible as a retelling of the early Naruto episodes. However, as a video game, while it does have its creative gimmicks, it fails to stick the landing on its very core mechanics.
The True Naruto Experience

This game looks incredible (for its time), and the way it merges the original anime with CG cutscenes is unlike anything I've ever seen before. I was not only impressed but also immersed, like I was watching this show again for the first time. The original anime soundtrack also contributes a lot to this sense of immersion. But it goes beyond just simple cinematic visuals. Many missions have uniquely crafted gameplay with custom objectives or quick time events that fit the tension and gravity of each moment. Even side missions are encouraged, beyond the usual currency and bonus rewards you get, because the more objectives you complete, the more villagers acknowledge Naruto (everyone hates him at the start). These villagers are physical NPCs spread throughout the village, and the more friendly NPCs you have, the easier it is to track down your next objective, and the less time you spend lost around Konoha.
Exploring The World

The star of this open world is definitely Konoha. The village is completely open, and the more you play, the more abilities you gain to explore it, like climbing up walls, grinding on the rails or even walking on water. Outside the village, paths tend to be a bit linear, which would stretch the definition of an open world by today's standards. Exploring this place is where things start to fall apart gameplay wise, as Naruto feels heavy when running around. The acceleration of the character is abrupt, the jump stops the momentum in a way that's not very natural or satisfying, and the controls feel delayed enough that it's easy to get frustrated when faced with tighter platforming challenges. Plus, your objectives are generally very dull, like collecting coins or completing some uninspired side missions. Overall, Naruto doesn't feel good to control, and that affects the entire exploration side of this game.
Fight Like a Ninja

Combat doesn't suffer from the same delay problems. Controls are responsive, characters have a decent number of combos to learn, and performing jutsu can be done by tilting both sticks in specific directions, emulating ninjutsu signs. Successfully performing a jutsu triggers a mini game and a cutscene that both the attacking and the defending player are competing against. These go from pressing a random button quicker than your opponent, to first person aiming or even button mashing. And if you get caught, you can escape a combo by spending a little bit of chakra, performing a substitution jutsu. This combat is fun... if you're playing it against the CPU. Against another player, things quickly devolve into a substitution festival, and on every opponent knockdown, you're going to see the same jutsu cutscenes repeated over and over, as that's the optimal way of dealing damage. It's a combat system that was definitely designed for single player first and multiplayer second. It can be fun to play with friends for a while, as long as neither player gets too good at it.

verdict: One of the greatest Naruto games ever made to immerse yourself in the Naruto story, and relive those first episodes, if you're willing to put up with some gameplay jank and suffer through some monotonous side activities.
